turns.content: Why does my custom file parser even implement the Read trait? I just tried adding it and got an error because I didn't know what to do with impl AsRef<str> or the underlying buffer. It feels like magic.

The confusion comes from mixing up the Read trait requirements with how you're actually using the data in your parser. The Read trait doesn't require you to implement AsRef<str>; that's a different interface used for string conversion which isn't part of the standard I/O pipeline when dealing with bytes directly. What matters is that your source type must provide the exact byte access that the Read trait expects, typically through methods like read_exact or peek.

But wait, I was told to implement Read, so why am I getting confused by this? If I just add impl Read<BufRead> to my struct and call read_exact, shouldn't it work immediately without any extra boilerplate? I'm spending too much time on the implementation details.

The issue is that you're conflating two different concepts: the trait bound itself versus the actual mechanism for reading data. Implementing Read for your custom source isn't enough; you also need to ensure your struct correctly wraps or delegates to a BufRead-compatible stream so that read_exact can actually extract bytes from it without failing due to missing capacity or buffer management.

I see, I was trying to use BufReader::lines on my own file wrapper but the lines function expects something specific. I thought chaining readers would just mean calling them in sequence like a normal loop, but I'm getting errors because I didn't understand how BufRead actually chains with other traits.

When you chain readers or try to use BufReader::lines on your custom source, you must ensure that every intermediate step respects the trait bounds required by those methods. The Read trait doesn't automatically grant access to lines; instead, you need a BufRead implementation that properly exposes its buffered stream so that methods like read_until can work correctly with your specific buffer handling.

Okay, but I still don't get why this matters for my real-world use case where I'm parsing log files. If I just wrap the file in a BufReader and call lines, shouldn't that be enough without worrying about the underlying trait implementation? Why does the Rust standard library force me to think so hard about these details?

The reason is that your custom source isn't always a direct wrapper around std::fs::File; sometimes you're building a pipe or network stream where the buffering strategy changes. If you skip understanding how Read and BufRead interact, your parser might crash when handling large files because read_exact assumes contiguous byte access while lines expects line-delimited data in a specific buffer format. The trait system forces you to explicitly choose whether you want raw bytes via Read or line-oriented parsing via BufRead, which determines how your application handles memory and performance.

I'm trying to build a custom input source in Rust, but I feel like it's just copying the pattern from std::io::BufRead which uses BufReader. Is implementing Read harder than wrapping an existing stream?

Implementing the Read trait is definitely more involved than using BufReader because you have to manage raw byte buffers and handle partial reads manually with read_exact or fill_buf. You need to implement the trait's methods directly instead of just delegating to an existing implementation.
I tried implementing it by calling BufRead::lines on a string stream, but that feels wrong. Should I be chaining multiple traits or is there a simpler way to delegate?

Chaining traits isn't the right approach here; you should implement Read by delegating to BufRead's methods internally using the std::io trait hierarchy. You can wrap your custom source in an adapter that implements Read, but avoid trying to chain multiple different traits together in one type.
Wait, if I just implement Read and call BufRead methods from inside it, isn't that technically chaining? Why is the tutorial saying not to do this?

The issue is that you shouldn't rely on the compiler automatically deriving or inferring the trait relationship; you must explicitly implement Read and then manually call BufRead methods from within your implementation. The tutorial warns against assuming automatic delegation because Rust requires explicit trait implementations rather than implicit chaining.

Hey, I'm stuck on implementing custom input in my project. I tried using serde_json::Value with impl Read but it's not working because the trait bounds are wrong. I thought if I just implement the trait for a struct that holds a Vec<u8>, I could read from it directly without extra steps.

You're right that you can implement Read for a custom type, but there's a specific way to do this with BufReader. If you wrap your custom source in an impl std::io::BufRead or use the BufRead trait methods like lines() instead of trying to call read_exact directly on a non-buffered stream, it handles buffering automatically and makes parsing much easier.
Ah okay. So I should probably wrap my struct in something that implements BufRead? But what if I want to use the Read trait specifically with read_exact for exact byte counts?

Exactly, you can chain them. You implement Read for your custom type and then wrap it in a BufReader so you get both the buffered behavior from lines() and the ability to use read_exact when needed. The key is that BufRead implements Read internally, so you don't need separate implementations if you're only reading lines.
Wait, does BufReader automatically handle the trait bounds? I was worried my struct needs to implement multiple traits or use a specific crate feature flag.

No extra features needed. Just ensure your struct holds data as Vec<u8> and implements Read, then wrap it in BufReader from std::io. The trait system handles the coercion so you can call methods on the wrapper without manually duplicating logic.
